problems.title: Grid Paths on a 6×6 Board
problems.question: You are playing a 2D game where you are trapped within a $6 \times 6$ grid and navigate via points on the grid. You start at the bottom left point $(0,0)$ and can only move up and right. How many possible paths can you take to reach the top right point $(6,6)$?
problems.hint: Every path is a sequence of 12 moves. How many of them must be "right"?
problems.solution:
Any path from $(0,0)$ to $(6,6)$ consists of exactly $6$ right moves and $6$ up moves, in some order — $12$ moves total. A path is fully determined by choosing which $6$ of the $12$ moves are to the right:
$$\binom{12}{6} = 924.$$

problems.title: Three-Card Straight from 8 Decks
problems.question: 8 standard 52-card decks are combined together and shuffled well. Find the probability that 3 cards drawn uniformly at random from this deck form a three-card straight (three consecutive ranks, with Ace counting as low or high). Straight flushes do not count.
problems.hint: There are 12 rank sequences (A-2-3 through Q-K-A). With 8 decks there are 32 cards of each rank — and remember to subtract the straight flushes.
problems.solution:
The combined deck has $416$ cards with $32$ cards of each rank ($8$ decks $\times\, 4$ suits). There are $12$ consecutive rank sequences: $A\text{-}2\text{-}3,\ 2\text{-}3\text{-}4,\ \dots,\ Q\text{-}K\text{-}A$.

For each sequence, pick one card of each rank: $32^3 = 32768$ ways. Subtract straight flushes — all three cards of the same suit: $4$ suits, and each specific card exists in $8$ copies, giving $4 \cdot 8^3 = 2048$.

$$P = \frac{12\,(32^3 - 4\cdot 8^3)}{\binom{416}{3}} = \frac{368640}{11912160} = \frac{768}{24817} \approx 0.0309.$$

problems.title: Four Dice Summing to 20
problems.question: Calculate the probability that when we roll 4 fair 6-sided dice, the sum of their upfaces is 20.
problems.hint: Replacing each face $x$ with $7-x$ turns "sum $= 20$" into "sum $= 8$", which is easier to count with stars and bars.
problems.solution:
Map each die $x_i \mapsto 7 - x_i$: this is a bijection, and the sum $20$ becomes $28 - 20 = 8$. So count solutions of $y_1 + y_2 + y_3 + y_4 = 8$ with $1 \le y_i \le 6$.

Substituting $z_i = y_i - 1$: $z_1+z_2+z_3+z_4 = 4$ with $z_i \ge 0$. Since $4 < 6$, the upper bound can never be violated, so stars and bars gives
$$\binom{4 + 3}{3} = \binom{7}{3} = 35.$$

$$P = \frac{35}{6^4} = \frac{35}{1296} \approx 0.0270.$$

problems.title: Counting the Digit 9 up to 1000
problems.question: How many times does the digit 9 occur when counting from 1 to 1000? Note that the number 919, for example, counts as two 9s.
problems.hint: Count position by position: how often is the units digit a 9? The tens digit? The hundreds digit?
problems.solution:
Consider the numbers $000$–$999$ as three-digit strings (adding $1000$ contributes no 9s). Each of the three positions cycles through $0$–$9$ uniformly, so each position shows a $9$ in exactly $\tfrac{1}{10}$ of the $1000$ numbers:
$$3 \times \frac{1000}{10} = 300.$$

problems.title: ±1 Walk Ending at 4
problems.question: Let $x_0 = 0$ and let $x_1, \dots, x_{10}$ satisfy $|x_i - x_{i-1}| = 1$ for $1 \le i \le 10$, with $x_{10} = 4$. How many such sequences are there?
problems.hint: Each step is $+1$ or $-1$. If there are $u$ up-steps and $d$ down-steps, what do $u + d$ and $u - d$ equal?
problems.solution:
Each step is $\pm 1$. With $u$ up-steps and $d$ down-steps: $u + d = 10$ and $u - d = 4$, so $u = 7$, $d = 3$. The walk is determined by choosing which $3$ of the $10$ steps go down:
$$\binom{10}{3} = 120.$$

problems.title: Binary Strings: Start 1 or End 00
problems.question: A computer is interpreting an 8-bit binary string, which consists of 8 characters that are either 1 or 0. How many such strings begin with 1 or end with the two characters 00?
problems.hint: Inclusion–exclusion: $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$.
problems.solution:
Let $A$ = strings starting with $1$: the other $7$ bits are free, $|A| = 2^7 = 128$. Let $B$ = strings ending in $00$: the other $6$ bits are free, $|B| = 2^6 = 64$. Both conditions fix $3$ bits: $|A \cap B| = 2^5 = 32$.
$$|A \cup B| = 128 + 64 - 32 = 160.$$

problems.title: Subsets with Bold Elements
problems.question: Erica intends to construct a subset $T$ of $S = \{I, J, K, L, M, N\}$, but if she is unsure about including an element $x$ of $S$ in $T$, she will write $x$ in bold and include it in $T$. For example, $\{I, \mathbf{J}\}$, $\{J, K, \mathbf{L}\}$, and $\{I, J, M, N\}$ are valid examples of $T$, while $\{I, J, \mathbf{J}, K\}$ is not. Find the total number of such sets $T$ that Erica can construct.
problems.hint: How many states can each element of $S$ independently be in?
problems.solution:
Each of the $6$ elements independently has $3$ states: excluded, included normally, or included in bold. (An element cannot appear both ways at once.)
$$3^6 = 729.$$

problems.title: Casino Triple Match
problems.question: A fair 6-sided die, a standard roulette wheel (38 slots: two zeroes and 1–36), and a standard deck of cards are in a casino. Letting Aces count as ones, what is the probability that when the roulette wheel is spun, the die is rolled, and a card is dealt, all of them show the same value?
problems.hint: The common value is forced to lie in $\{1, \dots, 6\}$. Sum the probability of a triple match over those values.
problems.solution:
The die restricts the common value $v$ to $\{1, \dots, 6\}$, and each such $v$ appears on the wheel and in the deck. For a fixed $v$:
$$P(\text{die} = v)\,P(\text{wheel} = v)\,P(\text{card} = v) = \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{38} \cdot \frac{4}{52}.$$
Summing over the $6$ values:
$$P = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{38} \cdot \frac{1}{13} = \frac{1}{494} \approx 0.00202.$$

problems.title: Age Order at a Round Table
problems.question: 7 people sit around a circular table uniformly at random. All of them have distinct ages. Find the probability that they sit in age order around the table. The ages can be increasing in either the clockwise or counter-clockwise direction.
problems.hint: Fix the youngest person's seat — how many arrangements of the rest are in order?
problems.solution:
There are $7!$ equally likely seatings. A valid "age order" arrangement is determined by where the youngest person sits ($7$ rotations) and the direction ($2$), giving $7 \times 2 = 14$ favorable seatings:
$$P = \frac{14}{7!} = \frac{14}{5040} = \frac{1}{360} \approx 0.00278.$$
Equivalently: seat people one at a time around the circle relative to the youngest — only $2$ of the $6!$ relative orders work.

problems.title: Charlie Between Two Boys
problems.question: There are 14 boys standing in a circle, one of whom is Charlie. 10 girls walk up and join the circle at random to form a larger circle, and each girl must stand between 2 boys. If all ways of forming the larger circle are equally likely, find the probability that Charlie is still standing between two boys.
problems.hint: The girls occupy 10 of the 14 gaps between adjacent boys. Which gaps must stay empty for Charlie?
problems.solution:
There are $14$ gaps between adjacent boys, and since every girl must end up between two boys, the $10$ girls occupy $10$ distinct gaps — all $\binom{14}{10}$ choices equally likely.

Charlie remains between two boys exactly when both gaps adjacent to him stay empty, i.e. the $10$ girls fit into the other $12$ gaps:
$$P = \frac{\binom{12}{10}}{\binom{14}{10}} = \frac{66}{1001} = \frac{6}{91} \approx 0.0659.$$

problems.title: A Weighted Hockey-Stick Sum
problems.question: Evaluate $$12\binom{3}{3} + 11\binom{4}{3} + 10\binom{5}{3} + \cdots + 2\binom{13}{3} + \binom{14}{3}.$$
problems.hint: Interpret each term as counting 5-element subsets of $\{1, \dots, 16\}$ classified by their fourth-smallest element.
problems.solution:
The sum is $\sum_{k=3}^{14} (15-k)\binom{k}{3}$. Count $5$-element subsets of $\{1, 2, \dots, 16\}$ by their fourth-smallest element $m$: we choose $3$ elements below it ($\binom{m-1}{3}$ ways) and $1$ element above it ($16 - m$ ways). Summing over $m$ and substituting $k = m - 1$ reproduces the sum exactly. Therefore
$$\sum_{k=3}^{14} (15-k)\binom{k}{3} = \binom{16}{5} = 4368.$$
(You can also telescope twice with the hockey-stick identity $\sum_{k=r}^{n}\binom{k}{r} = \binom{n+1}{r+1}$.)

problems.title: Pair-Flip Coin Game
problems.question: You flip 4 fair coins and observe the outcomes. You may then repeatedly turn over any 2 coins at a time (you cannot turn over just 1). Turning a coin flips it from heads to tails or tails to heads. Playing optimally to maximize heads, what is the expected number of heads you end up with?
problems.hint: What happens to the parity of the number of heads when you turn over two coins at once?
problems.solution:
Turning over two coins changes the number of heads by $-2$, $0$, or $+2$ — the parity of the head count is invariant.

If the initial number of heads is even ($0, 2, 4$), you can always reach $4$ heads (flip tails in pairs). If it is odd ($1, 3$), the best you can reach is $3$.

For $4$ fair coins, $P(\text{even}) = \frac{\binom{4}{0} + \binom{4}{2} + \binom{4}{4}}{16} = \frac{8}{16} = \frac12$, so
$$E[\text{heads}] = \frac12 \cdot 4 + \frac12 \cdot 3 = 3.5.$$

problems.title: Red, Green, Blue at a Corner
problems.question: The 6 faces of a cube are each painted a distinct color among red, blue, green, yellow, purple, and pink. Find the probability that the faces colored red, green, and blue all share a common vertex of the cube.
problems.hint: Three faces share a vertex exactly when no two of them are opposite faces. Count the face-triples at the cube's 8 corners.
problems.solution:
Three faces of a cube share a vertex if and only if they are pairwise adjacent (no two opposite). Each of the $8$ vertices determines one such triple, and all $8$ triples are distinct, so $8$ of the $\binom{6}{3} = 20$ equally likely face-triples work:
$$P = \frac{8}{20} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4.$$
Sequentially: green avoids the face opposite red with probability $\frac45$; then blue must avoid the faces opposite red and opposite green, $\frac24 = \frac12$ — and $\frac45 \cdot \frac12 = \frac25$.

problems.title: Arranging Colored Balls in a Line
problems.question: How many distinct ways can you arrange 3 red balls, 7 blue balls, and 9 green balls in a line? Balls of the same color are indistinguishable.
problems.hint: This is a multinomial coefficient — divide out the internal orderings of each color.
problems.solution:
With $19$ positions, choose which get red, blue, and green:
$$\binom{19}{3}\binom{16}{7} = \frac{19!}{3!\,7!\,9!} = 11{,}085{,}360.$$

problems.title: Matching Socks from a Drawer
problems.question: 10 pairs of socks, each pair a distinct color, are in a drawer. You draw 2 socks at random. Find the probability that you obtain a matching pair.
problems.hint: Condition on the first sock — how many of the remaining socks match it?
problems.solution:
Whatever the first sock is, exactly $1$ of the remaining $19$ socks matches it:
$$P = \frac{1}{19} \approx 0.0526.$$

problems.title: Lattice Paths in 3D
problems.question: What is the total number of paths from $(0,0,0)$ to $(3,4,5)$ in 3D space, given that at each step we can move one unit right, forward, or up?
problems.hint: Arrange a sequence of 12 moves: 3 of one kind, 4 of another, 5 of a third.
problems.solution:
A path is an arrangement of $3$ right, $4$ forward, and $5$ up moves — $12$ moves total:
$$\frac{12!}{3!\,4!\,5!} = 27{,}720.$$

problems.title: Handshakes Among Four Couples
problems.question: A room of four couples greet each other by shaking hands. If each person shakes the hand of every other person besides their partner, how many handshakes occur?
problems.hint: Count all possible handshakes among 8 people, then remove the ones that don't happen.
problems.solution:
Among $8$ people there are $\binom{8}{2} = 28$ possible handshakes. The $4$ within-couple handshakes don't occur:
$$28 - 4 = 24.$$

problems.title: Packing 3-Cubes into a 20-Cube
problems.question: How many $3 \times 3 \times 3$ cubes can fit inside a cube of dimensions $20 \times 20 \times 20$?
problems.hint: How many length-3 segments fit along one edge of length 20?
problems.solution:
Along each axis, $\lfloor 20/3 \rfloor = 6$ cubes fit, with $2$ units left over. Packing axis-aligned:
$$6 \times 6 \times 6 = 216.$$
The leftover $2$-unit slabs are too thin to hold another $3$-cube in that direction, so $216$ is the answer.

problems.title: Products Ending in 1, 3, 7, or 9
problems.question: Four numbers from $\{1, 2, \dots, 9\}$ are selected with repetition allowed. Find the probability that the product of the four integers has a units digit of $1$, $3$, $7$, or $9$.
problems.hint: A units digit of 1, 3, 7, or 9 means the product is coprime to 10. What does that force about each factor?
problems.solution:
The units digit is in $\{1, 3, 7, 9\}$ exactly when the product is divisible by neither $2$ nor $5$ — which requires every factor to be odd and not $5$. That leaves $\{1, 3, 7, 9\}$: $4$ of the $9$ choices for each draw, independently:
$$P = \left(\frac{4}{9}\right)^4 = \frac{256}{6561} \approx 0.0390.$$

problems.title: Exactly Two Aces in 13 Cards
problems.question: What is the probability of taking 13 cards from a well-shuffled standard 52-card deck and getting exactly 2 Aces? Round your answer to the nearest hundredth.
problems.hint: Hypergeometric: choose 2 of the 4 aces and 11 of the 48 non-aces.
problems.solution:
This is a hypergeometric probability — choose which $2$ of the $4$ aces and which $11$ of the $48$ non-aces land in the hand:
$$P = \frac{\binom{4}{2}\binom{48}{11}}{\binom{52}{13}} = \frac{4446}{20825} \approx 0.2135 \approx 0.21.$$

problems.title: Ruined on Schedule
problems.question: A gambler starts with $\$n$, where $n \ge 1$ is an integer. Each round, the gambler wins or loses $\$1$ with respective probabilities $p$ and $1-p$. Once the gambler has $\$0$ left, he leaves the table. The probability that the gambler is ruined after exactly $n + 2k$ rounds ($k \ge 0$ an integer) can be written as a function $f(p, n, k)$. Compute $f(1/3,\ 5,\ 3)$ to the nearest ten-thousandth.
problems.hint: This is a first-passage time. The hitting time theorem (or cycle lemma) says $P(T_{-n} = m) = \frac{n}{m}\,P(S_m = -n)$ for a random walk.
problems.solution:
Ruin at exactly $m = n + 2k$ rounds means the walk first hits $-n$ (net) at step $m$: that requires $k$ wins and $n + k$ losses. The hitting time theorem gives
$$P(T = m) = \frac{n}{m}\,P(S_m = -n) = \frac{n}{m}\binom{m}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n+k}.$$
With $p = 1/3$, $n = 5$, $k = 3$, $m = 11$:
$$\frac{5}{11}\binom{11}{3}\left(\frac13\right)^3\left(\frac23\right)^8 = \frac{6400}{59049} \approx 0.1084.$$
(Verified by simulation.) The $\frac{n}{m}$ factor is exactly the cycle-lemma correction that turns "ends at $-n$" into "first hits $-n$."

problems.title: The Ballot Problem
problems.question: Candidates $A$ and $B$ receive $n$ and $m$ votes respectively, with $n > m$. The $n + m$ ballots are shuffled and drawn one-by-one, keeping a running tally. The probability that $A$ is never behind in the count (ties allowed, excluding the initial $0$–$0$ state) is a function $Q(n, m)$. Find $Q(100, 80)$.
problems.hint: Map the count to a lattice path with $+1$/$-1$ steps and count paths that never go below $0$ using the reflection principle.
problems.solution:
Treat each $A$-vote as $+1$ and each $B$-vote as $-1$: we need the fraction of arrangements whose partial sums never go negative. By the reflection principle, the number of nonnegative paths with $n$ up-steps and $m$ down-steps is
$$\binom{n+m}{m} - \binom{n+m}{m-1},$$
so
$$Q(n,m) = 1 - \frac{\binom{n+m}{m-1}}{\binom{n+m}{m}} = 1 - \frac{m}{n+1} = \frac{n+1-m}{n+1}.$$
For $n = 100$, $m = 80$: $Q = \frac{21}{101} \approx 0.2079$.
(The strict version of this — $A$ always strictly ahead — is the classic ballot theorem $\frac{n-m}{n+m}$.)

problems.title: Meeting in the Final
problems.question: A single-elimination chess tournament has 128 players with distinct ratings, and the higher-rated player always wins. The bracket is drawn uniformly at random. What is the probability that the highest-rated and second-highest-rated players meet in the final?
problems.hint: They meet in the final if and only if they start in opposite halves of the bracket.
problems.solution:
The best player always reaches the final. The second-best reaches the final if and only if they are in the opposite half of the bracket from the best player — otherwise they meet earlier and #2 is eliminated.

Given #1's slot, the other $127$ slots are equally likely for #2, and $64$ of them lie in the opposite half:
$$P = \frac{64}{127} \approx 0.5039.$$

problems.title: Race to First Heads
problems.question: Bill and Bob are simultaneously flipping coins, each with probability $1/3$ of heads per flip. Find the probability that Bill obtains his first heads in strictly fewer flips than Bob.
problems.hint: Sum $P(T_{Bill} = k)\,P(T_{Bob} > k)$ over $k$ — both are geometric.
problems.solution:
With $p = \frac13$, $q = \frac23$, both times are Geometric($p$):
$$P(T_1 < T_2) = \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} p q^{k-1} \cdot q^{k} = \frac{pq}{1 - q^2} = \frac{(1/3)(2/3)}{1 - 4/9} = \frac{2}{5}.$$
Sanity check by symmetry: $P(T_1 < T_2) = P(T_2 < T_1)$ and $P(\text{tie}) = \frac{p^2 \cdot (\text{restart factor})}{\,} = \frac{p}{2-p} = \frac15$, and indeed $\frac25 + \frac25 + \frac15 = 1$.

problems.title: First to Two Heads
problems.question: Alice and Bob alternate tossing a fair coin, Alice first. The first person to accumulate two heads in total (not necessarily consecutive) wins. What is the probability that Alice wins?
problems.hint: First-step analysis on states $(a, b) = $ (Alice's heads, Bob's heads) at the start of Alice's turn. Solve from the endgame states backwards.
problems.solution:
Let $A_{ab}$ be the probability Alice wins when Alice has $a$ heads, Bob has $b$, and Alice is about to flip.

$A_{11} = \tfrac12 + \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12\cdot 0 + \tfrac12 A_{11}\right) \Rightarrow A_{11} = \tfrac23$.

$A_{10} = \tfrac12 + \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12 A_{11} + \tfrac12 A_{10}\right) \Rightarrow A_{10} = \tfrac89$.

$A_{01} = \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12\cdot 0 + \tfrac12 A_{11}\right) + \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12\cdot 0 + \tfrac12 A_{01}\right) \Rightarrow A_{01} = \tfrac29$.

$$A_{00} = \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12 A_{11} + \tfrac12 A_{10}\right) + \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12 A_{01} + \tfrac12 A_{00}\right) = \tfrac49 + \tfrac14 A_{00} \;\Rightarrow\; A_{00} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926.$$
Going first is worth about $9.3$ percentage points here.

problems.title: Monty Hall
problems.question: On a game show you pick one of three doors; one hides a car, two hide goats. The host — who knows where the car is — opens a door you did not pick, revealing a goat, and offers you the chance to switch to the other closed door. What is the probability you win the car if you switch?
problems.hint: Your original door was right with probability $1/3$. Does the host's (forced) reveal change that?
problems.solution:
Your initial pick is the car with probability $\frac13$, a goat with probability $\frac23$ — and the host's action can't change that, because he can always open a goat door regardless of your pick.

If your initial pick was a goat (probability $\frac23$), the host's reveal eliminates the *other* goat, so switching wins the car. If it was the car ($\frac13$), switching loses.
$$P(\text{win} \mid \text{switch}) = \frac23.$$
The key assumption doing the work: the host *knowingly* reveals a goat. If he opened a random unpicked door and happened to show a goat, the answer would be $\frac12$.

problems.title: Six Before Five
problems.question: Roll a fair 6-sided die until a 6 appears. Given that the first 6 occurs before the first 5, find the expected number of rolls.
problems.hint: The unconditional answer would be 6 — but conditioning on "no 5 before the 6" penalizes long sequences. Work out the conditional distribution of the number of rolls.
problems.solution:
For the first 6 to arrive on roll $t$ with no 5 appearing earlier, the first $t-1$ rolls must all land in $\{1,2,3,4\}$:
$$P(T = t,\ \text{6 before 5}) = \left(\frac46\right)^{t-1}\frac16, \qquad P(\text{6 before 5}) = \frac12.$$
So conditionally $P(T = t \mid \text{6 first}) = 2\left(\frac23\right)^{t-1}\frac16 = \left(\frac23\right)^{t-1}\frac13$ — geometric with success probability $\frac13$, giving
$$E[T \mid \text{6 before 5}] = 3.$$
It is *less* than 6: long games give the 5 more chances to show up first, so conditioning on "6 first" favors short games. (A very common interview trap is answering 6.)

problems.title: Neighboring Rolls
problems.question: On average, how many rolls of a fair 6-sided die are required to get two consecutive rolls that differ by at most 1?
problems.hint: Track only the last value rolled. By symmetry there are three kinds of states: $\{1,6\}$, $\{2,5\}$, $\{3,4\}$.
problems.solution:
After any roll, only the last value matters. Let $E_v$ be the expected additional rolls when the last roll was $v$. A last roll of $1$ or $6$ is "matched" by $2$ of the $6$ next values, $2$–$5$ by $3$ of $6$. By symmetry let $a = E_1 = E_6$, $b = E_2 = E_5$, $c = E_3 = E_4$:
$$a = 1 + \tfrac16(a + b + c + c),\quad b = 1 + \tfrac16(a + b + c),\quad c = 1 + \tfrac16(a + b),$$
(each equation keeps only the *non-matching* next values). Solving gives $a = \frac{72}{23},\ b = \frac{289}{115},\ c = \frac{252}{115}$, and the answer is
$$1 + \frac{2a + 2b + 2c}{6} = \frac{377}{115} \approx 3.278.$$
(Verified by simulation.)

problems.title: Average Chord Length
problems.question: Two points are selected uniformly at random on the circumference of the unit circle. Find the expected length of the chord between them.
problems.hint: Fix one point; the central angle $\theta$ to the other is uniform on $(0, 2\pi)$, and the chord has length $2\sin(\theta/2)$.
problems.solution:
By rotational symmetry, fix one endpoint; the central angle $\theta$ to the other is $\mathrm{Unif}(0, 2\pi)$, and the chord length is $2\sin(\theta/2)$:
$$E[L] = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi} 2\sin\!\left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) d\theta = \frac{1}{2\pi}\Big[-4\cos(\theta/2)\Big]_0^{2\pi} = \frac{8}{2\pi} = \frac{4}{\pi} \approx 1.273.$$

problems.title: Ramen Noodle Loops
problems.question: There are 100 noodles in your bowl of ramen. You repeatedly pick two loose ends uniformly at random and connect them, until no loose ends remain. On average how many circles (loops) do you create? Round to the nearest hundredth.
problems.hint: At each connection, what is the probability you close a loop, given how many loose ends remain? Then use linearity.
problems.solution:
When $2k$ loose ends remain, you pick one end; of the other $2k - 1$ ends, exactly one is the *other end of the same strand* — connecting to it closes a loop. So that step closes a loop with probability $\frac{1}{2k-1}$, independent of the past configuration.

By linearity over the 100 connection steps ($k = 100, 99, \dots, 1$):
$$E[\text{loops}] = \sum_{k=1}^{100} \frac{1}{2k-1} = 1 + \frac13 + \frac15 + \cdots + \frac{1}{199} \approx 3.28.$$
Remarkably small — 100 noodles yield only about 3 loops on average, and doubling the noodles adds roughly $\tfrac12\ln 2 \approx 0.35$ more.

problems.title: Uniform Sums Past ln 2
problems.question: Let $X_1, X_2, \dots$ be IID $\mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$ and let $N = \min\{n : X_1 + \cdots + X_n > \ln 2\}$. Find $E[N]$.
problems.hint: $P(N > n) = P(X_1 + \cdots + X_n \le t)$, and for $t \le 1$ that probability is $t^n/n!$.
problems.solution:
For $t \le 1$, the region $\{x_1 + \cdots + x_n \le t\}$ inside the unit cube is a simplex of volume $\frac{t^n}{n!}$, so $P(N > n) = \frac{t^n}{n!}$. Then
$$E[N] = \sum_{n \ge 0} P(N > n) = \sum_{n \ge 0} \frac{t^n}{n!} = e^{t}.$$
With $t = \ln 2$: $E[N] = e^{\ln 2} = 2$ — exactly.
(The famous special case $t = 1$ gives $E[N] = e$: on average you need $e$ uniforms to exceed a sum of 1.)

problems.title: Roll-or-Take on a d20
problems.question: You have a fair 20-sided die and 100 actions. Each action is either a roll (re-roll the current face) or a take (cash out the current face in dollars) — and you must roll again before each take. You fix a threshold $n$ in advance and take whenever the face is at least $n$. With the optimal threshold, what is your expected payout, rounded to the nearest whole dollar?
problems.hint: With threshold $n$, a success costs on average $\frac{1}{p} $ rolls plus 1 take, where $p = \frac{21-n}{20}$, and pays the conditional mean $\frac{n+20}{2}$. Balance value per action.
problems.solution:
With threshold $n$: each roll succeeds (face $\ge n$) with $p = \frac{21-n}{20}$, and a successful face averages $\bar{v} = \frac{n+20}{2}$. Each "cycle" (roll until success, then take) consumes $\frac{1}{p} + 1$ actions on average and pays $\bar{v}$, so the payout rate per action is roughly
$$\frac{\bar v\, p}{1 + p}.$$
Maximizing over integer $n$ (or exactly, via the dynamic program $V_a = p(\bar v + V_{a-2}) + (1-p)V_{a-1}$ over the 100 actions) gives the optimum at $n = 6$:
$$E[\text{payout}] \approx \$553.96 \approx \$554.$$
Low thresholds waste takes on small faces; high thresholds waste rolls hunting for rare ones. $n=6$ takes about $\tfrac34$ of rolls at an average of $13$ per take.

problems.title: Three Heads or Three Tails
problems.question: Anna and Brenda repeatedly toss a fair coin. Anna wins if 3 heads appear in a row; Brenda wins if 3 tails appear in a row. What is the expected number of tosses until someone wins?
problems.hint: By symmetry, track only the length of the current run (1 or 2). Two equations, two unknowns.
problems.solution:
By heads/tails symmetry, the state is just the current run length. Let $E_1, E_2$ be the expected remaining tosses with a run of 1 or 2 (of either symbol):
$$E_1 = 1 + \tfrac12 E_2 + \tfrac12 E_1, \qquad E_2 = 1 + \tfrac12 \cdot 0 + \tfrac12 E_1$$
(continuing the run advances it; switching symbols starts a new run of 1). The first equation gives $E_1 = 2 + E_2$; substituting, $E_1 = 3 + \tfrac12 E_1$, so $E_1 = 6$.

Adding the very first toss (which always starts a run of 1):
$$E = 1 + E_1 = 7.$$

problems.title: Dollar Re-Rolls
problems.question: Alice rolls a fair 6-sided die. After seeing the face she may stop and receive its value in dollars, or pay $\$1$ to roll again — with no limit on re-rolls. Under optimal play, what is her expected payout?
problems.hint: Let $V$ be the value of the game. Then $V = \frac16\sum_f \max(f,\ V - 1)$ — solve the fixed point.
problems.solution:
Let $V$ be the optimal value. After a roll showing $f$, Alice takes the better of stopping ($f$) or paying $\$1$ to be back at the start ($V - 1$):
$$V = \frac16\sum_{f=1}^{6} \max(f,\ V-1).$$
Guess the accept set $\{4,5,6\}$ (i.e. $3 \le V-1 \le 4$): $V = \frac16(3 \cdot (V-1) + 4 + 5 + 6) = \frac{V-1}{2} + \frac{15}{6}$, giving $V = 4$ — consistent, since $V - 1 = 3$ exactly. So Alice re-rolls on 1, 2 (and is indifferent on 3), and the game is worth
$$V = \$4.$$

problems.title: Penney's Game: HTH vs HHT
problems.question: You and a friend toss a fair coin repeatedly. You win if the pattern HTH appears before HHT; otherwise your friend wins. What is the probability your friend wins?
problems.hint: What happens once HH has appeared? One of the two patterns can no longer lose from there.
problems.solution:
Key observation: once $HH$ appears, your friend is certain to win — from $HH$, tails completes $HHT$, and heads keeps the state at $HH$, so $HHT$ arrives before any $HTH$ can form.

Let $a$ be your (HTH) win probability from state "$H$" (fresh head, no progress beyond it). From $H$: heads ($\tfrac12$) reaches $HH$ — you lose; tails reaches $HT$, from which heads ($\tfrac12$) completes $HTH$ — you win — and tails ($\tfrac12$) resets to no-progress, which returns to state $H$ when the next head arrives (worth $a$):
$$a = \tfrac12\cdot 0 + \tfrac12\left(\tfrac12\cdot 1 + \tfrac12\,a\right) \;\Rightarrow\; a = \tfrac13.$$
The game always begins by reaching state $H$, so you win with probability $\tfrac13$ and your friend wins with probability
$$\frac{2}{3}.$$
This non-transitivity (every pattern is beaten by some other) is Penney's game.

problems.title: Ant on a Cube
problems.question: An ant stands on a vertex of a cube and at each step moves to one of the three adjacent vertices with equal probability. Find the expected number of steps for the ant to return to its starting vertex.
problems.hint: For a random walk on a regular graph, the stationary distribution is uniform — and expected return time is $1/\pi_v$.
problems.solution:
The cube graph is 3-regular, so the random walk's stationary distribution is uniform over the $8$ vertices: $\pi_v = \frac18$. For any irreducible Markov chain, the expected return time to a state is the reciprocal of its stationary probability:
$$E[\text{return}] = \frac{1}{\pi_v} = 8.$$
No system of equations needed — this works for any regular graph (return time = number of vertices).

problems.title: First Ace
problems.question: Cards are flipped one at a time from a shuffled standard 52-card deck. On average, how many cards must you flip to see the first ace?
problems.hint: The 4 aces split the other 48 cards into 5 gaps of equal expected size.
problems.solution:
By symmetry, the $4$ aces divide the $48$ non-aces into $5$ gaps (before the first ace, between aces, after the last), and each non-ace is equally likely to fall in any gap. So the expected number of non-aces before the first ace is $\frac{48}{5}$, and
$$E = \frac{48}{5} + 1 = \frac{53}{5} = 10.6.$$
General form: first of $k$ special cards in a deck of $n$ appears at expected position $\frac{n+1}{k+1}$.

problems.title: Crossing Chords
problems.question: 10 chords are drawn on a circle, each with both endpoints chosen uniformly at random on the circumference. What is the expected number of intersection points?
problems.hint: For any two chords, their 4 endpoints are in uniformly random positions around the circle. When do two chords cross?
problems.solution:
Take any two chords: their $4$ endpoints are $4$ uniformly random points on the circle. The chords cross exactly when the endpoints alternate around the circle — $1$ of the $3$ equally likely pairings of $4$ points into two chords. So each pair of chords crosses with probability $\frac13$, and by linearity:
$$E[\text{intersections}] = \binom{10}{2}\cdot\frac13 = \frac{45}{3} = 15.$$

problems.title: Collecting All Four Suits
problems.question: Audrey repeatedly draws cards from a standard 52-card deck with replacement. What is the expected number of draws until she has seen at least one card of each suit?
problems.hint: Coupon collector with 4 coupons: how long does each "new suit" stage take?
problems.solution:
This is the coupon-collector problem with $4$ equally likely suits. When $k$ suits are still missing, each draw finds a new one with probability $\frac{k}{4}$, taking $\frac{4}{k}$ draws in expectation:
$$E = \frac44 + \frac43 + \frac42 + \frac41 = 4\left(1 + \tfrac12 + \tfrac13 + \tfrac14\right) = \frac{25}{3} \approx 8.33.$$

problems.title: Risky Second Roll
problems.question: You roll a fair 6-sided die. You may keep the roll and receive its value in dollars — or roll again, in which case: if the two rolls sum to at least 7 you pay your first roll's value, and if the sum is less than 7 you receive the second roll's value. Under optimal play, what is your expected payout?
problems.hint: Compute the re-roll EV as a function of your first roll $f$ — note that higher $f$ both shrinks the safe range and raises the penalty.
problems.solution:
After a first roll of $f$, a second roll of $s$ is safe when $s \le 6 - f$ (paying $s$) and costly otherwise (paying $-f$, which happens for $f$ of the six values):
$$EV_{re}(f) = \frac{1}{6}\left[\frac{(6-f)(7-f)}{2} - f^2\right].$$
Evaluating: $EV_{re}(1) = \frac{7}{3} > 1$, but $EV_{re}(2) = 1 < 2$, and it only gets worse for higher $f$. So the only profitable re-roll is on a first roll of 1:
$$E = \frac16\cdot\frac73 + \frac{2+3+4+5+6}{6} = \frac{7}{18} + \frac{20}{6} = \frac{67}{18} \approx 3.72.$$
High first rolls are doubly bad to re-roll: the safe window shrinks *and* the forfeit grows.

problems.title: A Fibonacci Telescope
problems.question: Let $F_n$ be the $n$th Fibonacci number ($F_1 = F_2 = 1$). Compute $$\frac{F_1 + F_2 + F_3 + \cdots + F_{300}}{F_3 + F_6 + F_9 + \cdots + F_{300}}.$$
problems.hint: Both sums telescope: $\sum_{k=1}^n F_k = F_{n+2} - 1$, and try expressing $F_{3k}$ as $\tfrac12(F_{3k+2} - F_{3k-1})$.
problems.solution:
The full sum telescopes by the standard identity:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{300} F_k = F_{302} - 1.$$
For the every-third sum, note $F_{3k} = \tfrac12\left(F_{3k+2} - F_{3k-1}\right)$ (check: both sides satisfy it since $F_{3k+2} = F_{3k+1} + F_{3k} = 2F_{3k} + F_{3k-1}$). Telescoping:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{100} F_{3k} = \frac{F_{302} - F_2}{2} = \frac{F_{302} - 1}{2}.$$
The ratio is exactly
$$\frac{F_{302} - 1}{(F_{302} - 1)/2} = 2.$$
Equivalently: every third Fibonacci sum captures exactly half of the total — a consequence of $F_{3k}$ being precisely the even Fibonacci numbers.

problems.title: First Roll Lower
problems.question: You roll a fair 6-sided die twice. Calculate the probability that the value of the first roll is strictly less than the value of the second roll.
problems.hint: Use symmetry between the two rolls — after removing ties, what's left splits evenly.

problems.title: LOLLIPOP, Bookended
problems.question: How many distinguishable permutations of $LOLLIPOP$ are there that start and end with the same letter? (E.g. $LOLIPOPL$ is valid, as it starts and ends with L.)
problems.hint: Only letters with at least two copies can bookend. Case on which letter it is and permute the remaining multiset.

problems.title: Another Matching Pair
problems.question: Marissa has a deck of 40 cards: four 1's, four 2's, ..., four 10's. A matching pair (2 cards of the same number) is removed from the deck. If Marissa randomly draws 2 more cards, what is the probability that she draws another matching pair?
problems.hint: After the removal, one rank has 2 cards left and nine ranks still have 4. Count matching pairs in the 38 remaining cards.
problems.solution:
Whichever rank the removed pair had, the remaining $38$ cards consist of one rank with $2$ copies and nine ranks with $4$ copies. The number of matching pairs available is
$$\binom{2}{2} + 9\binom{4}{2} = 1 + 54 = 55,$$
out of $\binom{38}{2} = 703$ equally likely draws:
$$P = \frac{55}{703} \approx 0.0782.$$

problems.title: The Heaviest Weight's Side
problems.question: We have 6 weights of mass $201, 202, \dots, 206$ grams. We randomly place 3 weights on each side of a scale. Find the probability that the side holding the 206-gram weight is heavier.
problems.hint: Condition on which two weights join 206. The total is $1221$, so 206's side wins iff its trio sums to at least $611$.
problems.solution:
The total mass is $1221$, so 206's side is heavier iff its three weights sum to $\ge 611$ (ties are impossible: $610.5$ isn't an integer), i.e. iff 206's two partners sum to $\ge 405$.

The $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ equally likely partner pairs from $\{201,\dots,205\}$ have sums $403, 404, 405, \dots, 409$ — only $\{201,202\}$ (sum $403$) and $\{201,203\}$ (sum $404$) fail:
$$P = \frac{8}{10} = 0.8.$$

problems.title: Tower of 1s and 2s
problems.question: You're building a tower 12 blocks tall using blocks of height 1 or height 2 (unlimited supply of each). How many different tower configurations are possible?
problems.hint: Let $a_n$ count towers of height $n$. What are the two ways a tower of height $n$ can end?
problems.solution:
A height-$n$ tower ends in either a 1-block (on a height-$(n{-}1)$ tower) or a 2-block (on a height-$(n{-}2)$ tower):
$$a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2}, \qquad a_1 = 1,\ a_2 = 2.$$
This is the Fibonacci sequence shifted: $a_n = F_{n+1}$. So $a_{12} = F_{13} = 233$.

problems.title: The Third Shiny Penny
problems.question: There are 3 shiny and 4 dull pennies in a box. Pennies are drawn one at a time without replacement. Compute the probability that it takes more than 4 draws for the third shiny penny to appear.
problems.hint: Complement: the third shiny appears within the first 4 draws. Where can the shinies sit among the first four positions?
problems.solution:
Work with the complement — all $3$ shinies among the first $4$ positions. All $\binom{7}{3} = 35$ placements of the shiny positions are equally likely, and $\binom{4}{3} = 4$ of them put all three in the first four draws:
$$P(T \le 4) = \frac{4}{35} \quad\Rightarrow\quad P(T > 4) = \frac{31}{35} \approx 0.886.$$

problems.title: Before the Two of Hearts
problems.question: A well-shuffled deck of cards is dealt one by one until the two of hearts appears. Find the probability that no kings, queens, or jacks appear before the two of hearts.
problems.hint: Only 13 cards matter: the two of hearts and the 12 face cards. What must be true of their relative order?
problems.solution:
Ignore the other 39 cards entirely — the event depends only on the relative order of the two of hearts and the $12$ face cards. All $13!$ orderings of those cards are equally likely, and we need the two of hearts first among them:
$$P = \frac{1}{13} \approx 0.0769.$$

problems.title: Exactly One Consecutive Pair
problems.question: How many subsets of $\{1, 2, \dots, 10\}$ contain exactly one pair of consecutive integers? For example, $\{3, 5, 6, 9\}$ contains exactly one such pair ($5,6$), while $\{1, 2, 3\}$ contains two.
problems.hint: Model the subset as one "domino" (the consecutive pair) plus isolated singletons, with gaps of at least one between all items.
problems.solution:
Such a subset is one block of two consecutive integers (a domino) plus $t$ isolated elements, with every pair of chosen items separated by a gap of at least $1$ (so no extra adjacencies form).

Placing $t+1$ items (sizes $2, 1, \dots, 1$) in a row of $10$ with internal gaps $\ge 1$ leaves $10 - (t+2) - t$ free slack; distributing it gives $\binom{9-t}{t+1}$ position patterns, times $(t+1)$ choices of which item is the domino:
$$\sum_{t=0}^{4} (t+1)\binom{9-t}{t+1} = 9 + 56 + 105 + 60 + 5 = 235.$$
(Verified by direct enumeration of all $2^{10}$ subsets.)

problems.title: Turtle Rendezvous
problems.question: Two turtles, Tort and Bort, perform independent simple symmetric random walks on the integers, starting at positions 0 and 4 respectively. Compute the probability that after 10 steps each, Tort and Bort are at the same position.
problems.hint: Look at the difference of the two walks — it's itself a random walk with twice the steps.
problems.solution:
Consider $D = $ (Tort's position) $-$ (Bort's position), which starts at $-4$. Each turtle's step changes $D$ by $\pm 1$ independently, so after both walk 10 steps, $D$ has moved by the sum of $20$ IID $\pm 1$ steps. They meet iff that sum is $+4$, i.e. $12$ ups and $8$ downs:
$$P = \frac{\binom{20}{8}}{2^{20}} = \frac{125970}{1048576} \approx 0.1201.$$

problems.title: The Cashier's Change
problems.question: 38 people are waiting to see a tennis match with a $\$5$ entrance fee. 19 of them have only $\$10$ bills and 19 have only $\$5$ bills, in uniformly random order, and the cashier starts with no change. What is the probability the cashier can always make change (a $\$10$ payer always receives a $\$5$ bill back)?
problems.hint: Track (number of $\$5$s received) minus (number of $\$10$s received) as the line progresses — it must never go negative. Same machinery as the ballot problem.
problems.solution:
Each $\$5$ payer is a $+1$ (a five enters the till), each $\$10$ payer a $-1$ (a five leaves). The cashier survives iff the running sum never goes negative — a nonnegative lattice path with $n = 19$ ups and $m = 19$ downs.

By the reflection principle (weak ballot problem):
$$P = \frac{n + 1 - m}{n + 1} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05.$$
With equal counts, survival is just $\frac{1}{n+1}$ — the Catalan fraction $\frac{C_n \cdot \binom{2n}{n}^{-1}}{}$ in disguise: of all $\binom{38}{19}$ orderings, a Catalan-number's worth are safe.

problems.title: An Ace for Everyone
problems.question: A dealer shuffles a fair 52-card deck and deals 13 cards to each of 4 players. What is the probability that every player receives exactly one ace?
problems.hint: Track only where the 4 aces land — the positions of the aces among the four hands are what matter.
problems.solution:
Deal the aces one at a time and track which hands they land in. The first ace lands anywhere; the second must land in one of the $39$ slots of the other three hands out of $51$ remaining; and so on:
$$P = \frac{52}{52}\cdot\frac{39}{51}\cdot\frac{26}{50}\cdot\frac{13}{49} = \frac{4!\,\cdot 13^4}{52\cdot 51\cdot 50\cdot 49} = \frac{2197}{20825} \approx 0.1055.$$
Only about a 1-in-9.5 chance — rarer than most people's intuition suggests.

problems.title: The Last Cherry
problems.question: Amy has a bowl of 5 red cherries and 8 purple cherries. She takes out cherries one at a time, uniformly at random, until there are no red cherries left. What is the probability that the bowl is then empty?
problems.hint: The bowl ends empty exactly when a particular cherry is drawn last. Which one?
problems.solution:
Amy stops when the fifth red cherry is drawn. The bowl is empty at that moment iff the very last cherry in the random order is red. By symmetry, each of the $13$ cherries is equally likely to be last:
$$P = \frac{5}{13} \approx 0.3846.$$

problems.title: Boy–Girl Neighbors
problems.question: There are 15 boys and 10 girls in a class. They line up in a row in random order. What is the expected number of adjacent boy–girl pairs? (E.g. $BGBBGGGBGGBBBBG$ has 7 such adjacencies.)
problems.hint: Linearity over the 24 adjacent position-pairs: what is the probability any given pair is mixed?
problems.solution:
There are $24$ adjacent position pairs. Any given pair is boy–girl (in either order) with probability
$$2\cdot\frac{15}{25}\cdot\frac{10}{24} = \frac12,$$
so by linearity of expectation
$$E = 24 \cdot \frac12 = 12.$$
No independence needed — linearity doesn't care that adjacent pairs overlap.

problems.title: Tails Come in Pairs
problems.question: A fair coin is flipped 10 times. Find the probability that tails only occur in consecutive pairs — i.e., every maximal run of tails has even length. (With 4 flips: $TTHH$, $TTTT$, $HHHH$ are valid; $THTH$ is not.)
problems.hint: Valid sequences are exactly tilings of a length-10 strip by $H$ tiles (length 1) and $TT$ tiles (length 2).
problems.solution:
A valid sequence decomposes uniquely into blocks $H$ (length 1) and $TT$ (length 2) — even tail-runs are just stacked $TT$ blocks. So valid sequences of length $n$ are tilings counted by
$$a_n = a_{n-1} + a_{n-2}, \qquad a_0 = a_1 = 1,$$
the Fibonacci numbers: $a_{10} = F_{11} = 89$. Hence
$$P = \frac{89}{2^{10}} = \frac{89}{1024} \approx 0.0869.$$
(Direct enumeration of all 1024 sequences confirms 89.)

problems.title: Labeling a Blank Die
problems.question: How many distinct ways can you label the 6 faces of a blank die with the numbers 1–6? Labelings that differ by a rotation of the die are not considered distinct.
problems.hint: Count all labelings, then divide by the number of rotations of a cube.
problems.solution:
There are $6!$ ways to place the labels, and the cube's rotation group has $24$ elements ($6$ faces can point down, $4$ rotations each). Since every rotation produces a distinct labeling (no labeling is rotation-symmetric when all faces differ):
$$\frac{6!}{24} = \frac{720}{24} = 30.$$

problems.title: The Missing Seven
problems.question: 7 people each independently select a uniformly random integer from $\{1, 2, \dots, 7\}$. Given that nobody selected 7, find the probability that 7 is the only value nobody selected.
problems.hint: Conditioned on avoiding 7, the picks are IID uniform on $\{1,\dots,6\}$ — so you need the probability that 7 draws cover all 6 values (count surjections).
problems.solution:
Given no one picked 7, the seven picks are IID uniform on $\{1, \dots, 6\}$. "7 is the only missing value" now means all of $1$–$6$ appear among the 7 picks — the picks form a surjection onto $\{1,\dots,6\}$.

A surjection from 7 elements onto 6 doubles up exactly one value: choose the doubled value ($6$), choose which two picks form the double ($\binom{7}{2} = 21$), and assign the rest ($5!$):
$$\frac{6 \cdot 21 \cdot 120}{6^7} = \frac{15120}{279936} = \frac{35}{648} \approx 0.054.$$

problems.title: Non-Decreasing Codes
problems.question: Pizza Hut gives new users a 7-digit code. Digits may repeat and codes may start with 0. How many such codes have non-decreasing digits?
problems.hint: A non-decreasing string is fully determined by how many times each digit appears — stars and bars.
problems.solution:
A non-decreasing code is determined entirely by its multiset of digits: choose how many of each digit $0$–$9$ appears, with the counts summing to $7$. By stars and bars:
$$\binom{7 + 10 - 1}{7} = \binom{16}{7} = 11440.$$

problems.title: Ten Lines, Many Regions
problems.question: Determine the maximum number of regions into which the plane can be divided by 10 lines, no two parallel. (A single line divides the plane into 2 regions.)
problems.hint: When the $k$th line is added, each intersection it makes with earlier lines splits one more region.
problems.solution:
Adding the $k$th line in general position crosses the $k-1$ existing lines in $k-1$ points, splitting the new line into $k$ pieces — each piece cuts one region in two, adding $k$ regions. Starting from $1$ region:
$$R(n) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{n} k = 1 + n + \binom{n}{2}.$$
For $n = 10$: $1 + 10 + 45 = 56$.

problems.title: Exactly One Pair
problems.question: A poker hand is five cards from a standard 52-card deck. What is the probability of a single pair — two cards of the same rank, with the other three cards of three different other ranks?
problems.hint: Build the hand: pick the paired rank, its two suits, then three distinct other ranks and one suit each.
problems.solution:
Count the favorable hands directly:
$$\underbrace{13}_{\text{pair rank}}\cdot\underbrace{\binom{4}{2}}_{\text{pair suits}}\cdot\underbrace{\binom{12}{3}}_{\text{other ranks}}\cdot\underbrace{4^3}_{\text{their suits}} = 1{,}098{,}240,$$
out of $\binom{52}{5} = 2{,}598{,}960$ hands:
$$P = \frac{1098240}{2598960} = \frac{352}{833} \approx 0.4226.$$
Nearly a coin flip — one pair is the most common made hand in poker.

problems.title: Sum of 4 Before Sum of 2
problems.question: Jed rolls two fair 6-sided dice repeatedly. Find the probability that Jed obtains a sum of 4 before a sum of 2.
problems.hint: All other sums are irrelevant — condition on the first roll that is a 4 or a 2.
problems.solution:
Rolls that are neither sum restart the situation, so only the first "relevant" roll matters. A sum of 4 has $3$ of $36$ outcomes, a sum of 2 has $1$:
$$P = \frac{3/36}{3/36 + 1/36} = \frac{3}{4}.$$
This "race between events" reduction — ignore everything that isn't one of the two targets — is worth internalizing; it reappears in craps-style problems constantly.

problems.title: Median of Three Uniforms
problems.question: Three numbers are drawn independently from $\mathrm{Unif}(0,1)$ and sorted. What is the probability that the median is less than $2/3$?
problems.hint: The median is below $m$ iff at least two of the three draws are below $m$.
problems.solution:
The median is $< m$ iff at least $2$ of the $3$ IID draws are $< m$. With each draw below $m$ with probability $m$:
$$P = \binom{3}{2}m^2(1-m) + m^3 = 3m^2 - 2m^3.$$
At $m = \tfrac23$: $3\cdot\tfrac49 - 2\cdot\tfrac{8}{27} = \tfrac{20}{27} \approx 0.7407.$

problems.title: Three Ants on a Triangle
problems.question: Three ants sit at the three vertices of an equilateral triangle. Each simultaneously picks one of its two adjacent vertices uniformly at random and walks along the edge toward it. What is the probability that no two ants collide?
problems.hint: For no collisions, the ants must all rotate the same way.
problems.solution:
If any two ants walk toward each other along the same edge, they collide; two ants aiming at the same vertex also meet. The only collision-free outcomes are all three moving clockwise, or all three counter-clockwise — $2$ of the $2^3 = 8$ equally likely choices:
$$P = \frac{2}{8} = \frac14.$$

problems.title: Did Gabe Know It?
problems.question: On a multiple-choice exam with 5 options per question, Gabe either knows the answer (and answers correctly) or guesses uniformly at random. The probability he knows the answer to any question is $0.6$. Given that an answer was correct, find the probability that he knew it.
problems.hint: Bayes: compare the two ways an answer ends up correct.
problems.solution:
$$P(\text{knew} \mid \text{correct}) = \frac{0.6 \cdot 1}{0.6 \cdot 1 + 0.4 \cdot \tfrac15} = \frac{0.6}{0.68} = \frac{15}{17} \approx 0.882.$$
Guessing correctly is rare enough ($0.08$ overall) that a correct answer is strong evidence of knowledge.

problems.title: Coin-Flip Bankruptcy
problems.question: Vishnu and Jason have $\$1000$ and $\$500$ in their accounts. They flip a fair coin repeatedly: heads, Vishnu pays Jason $\$1$; tails, Jason pays Vishnu $\$1$. What is the probability that Jason runs out of money first?
problems.hint: Fair gambler's ruin: ruin probabilities are proportional to the *opponent's* bankroll.
problems.solution:
For a fair game, the classic gambler's-ruin result: starting with $a$ against an opponent with $b$, you go broke with probability $\frac{b}{a+b}$. (The process is a martingale — expected final wealth must equal starting wealth.)

Jason starts with $500$ against $1000$:
$$P(\text{Jason ruined}) = \frac{1000}{1500} = \frac23.$$

problems.title: Alternating Die Duel
problems.question: Two players alternate rolling a die, Player 1 first. Player 1 wins by rolling a 1 or 2 on his turn; Player 2 wins by rolling a 3, 4, 5, or 6 on his turn. The game continues until someone wins. If $p_1$ and $p_2$ are the players' winning probabilities, find $\max\{p_1, p_2\}$.
problems.hint: First-step analysis: $p_1 = \frac13 + \frac23\cdot\frac13\,p_1$.
problems.solution:
Player 1 wins immediately with probability $\tfrac13$; otherwise (prob $\tfrac23$) Player 2 wins with probability $\tfrac23$, and if both miss (prob $\tfrac23\cdot\tfrac13$) the game restarts:
$$p_1 = \frac13 + \frac23\cdot\frac13\,p_1 \;\Rightarrow\; p_1 = \frac{1/3}{7/9} = \frac37, \qquad p_2 = \frac47.$$
Despite moving second, Player 2's much higher per-turn win rate makes him the favorite: $\max = \frac47 \approx 0.571$.

problems.title: Convincingly Double-Headed
problems.question: A bag has 19 fair coins and 1 double-headed coin. You pick a coin uniformly at random and flip it. What is the minimum number of consecutive heads you must observe before the coin is more likely than not to be the double-headed one?
problems.hint: After $n$ heads, compare the prior odds $1{:}19$ against the likelihood ratio $2^n$.
problems.solution:
In odds form: prior odds of double-headed are $1{:}19$, and each observed head multiplies the odds by $2$ (likelihood ratio of heads: $1$ vs $\tfrac12$). After $n$ heads the odds are $2^n : 19$, which favor the double-headed coin once $2^n > 19$:
$$n = 5 \quad (2^5 = 32 > 19,\ \text{posterior} \approx 0.627).$$
Four heads gives odds $16:19$ — still more likely a fair coin.

problems.title: Six Heads from a Random Coin
problems.question: A bag contains biased coins whose heads-probability $T$ has density $f(t) = 2t$ on $(0,1)$. You draw a coin at random and toss it 6 times, winning if all 6 tosses are heads. What is your probability of winning?
problems.hint: Condition on the coin: $P(\text{win}) = E[T^6]$ — integrate against the density.
problems.solution:
Conditioning on the coin's bias $T = t$, the win probability is $t^6$. Averaging over the bag:
$$P = E[T^6] = \int_0^1 t^6 \cdot 2t\,dt = 2\int_0^1 t^7\,dt = \frac{2}{8} = \frac14.$$
Far higher than $\left(E[T]\right)^6 = (2/3)^6 \approx 0.088$ — the heavy right tail of $f$ dominates, a good reminder that $E[T^6] \ne E[T]^6$.

problems.title: Bertrand's Bowls
problems.question: Three bowls: one holds two blue balls, one holds two red balls, one holds one of each. You pick a bowl uniformly at random and draw a ball — it's blue. You replace it and draw again from the same bowl. What is the probability the second draw is also blue?
problems.hint: The first blue draw changes the odds of which bowl you're holding — update first, then predict.
problems.solution:
Posterior on the bowls after one blue draw: the all-blue bowl has likelihood $1$, the mixed bowl $\tfrac12$, the all-red bowl $0$ — so posterior odds $2{:}1$ for the all-blue bowl.
$$P(\text{blue again}) = \frac23 \cdot 1 + \frac13 \cdot \frac12 = \frac56 \approx 0.833.$$
The naive answer $\tfrac12$ ("replacement makes draws independent") ignores that both draws share the same unknown bowl.

problems.title: An Even Number of Flips
problems.question: Ahmed flips a fair coin until he obtains two consecutive identical outcomes (HH or TT) for the first time. What is the probability that Ahmed flips the coin an even number of times?
problems.hint: Stopping at flip $k$ means the first $k-1$ flips strictly alternate. What's the probability of that?
problems.solution:
Stopping at exactly flip $k \ge 2$ requires the first $k-1$ flips to alternate ($2$ ways) and flip $k$ to repeat flip $k-1$:
$$P(T = k) = \frac{2}{2^{k}}\cdot 1 = 2^{1-k}.$$
Summing over even $k$:
$$P = \sum_{j\ge 1} 2^{1-2j} = \frac{1/2}{1 - 1/4} = \frac23.$$

problems.title: One Extra Coin
problems.question: Emilia has $n+1$ fair coins and Ron has $n$ fair coins. Both flip all of their coins. What is the probability that Emilia flips strictly more heads than Ron?
problems.hint: Set Emilia's last coin aside and use the symmetry between "more heads" and "more tails" on the rest.
problems.solution:
Compare their first $n$ coins each. By symmetry, $P(\text{Emilia ahead}) = P(\text{Ron ahead}) = p$ and $P(\text{tied}) = t$, with $2p + t = 1$.

Emilia wins overall if she's already ahead (her extra coin can't hurt: probability $p$), or if they're tied and her extra coin lands heads ($t\cdot\tfrac12$):
$$P = p + \frac{t}{2} = p + \frac{1 - 2p}{2} = \frac12,$$
regardless of $n$. The extra coin exactly breaks the tie symmetry — no computation needed.

problems.title: Fair Meets Loaded
problems.question: Emma rolls two dice: one fair, and one loaded so that each face appears with probability proportional to its value. Find the probability that the sum of the two dice is odd.
problems.hint: You only need the parity of each die. What does the fair die contribute to the parity of the sum?
problems.solution:
The sum is odd iff the two dice have different parities. The fair die is odd with probability exactly $\tfrac12$, so whatever the loaded die's parity turns out to be, the fair die "disagrees" with it with probability $\tfrac12$:
$$P(\text{odd sum}) = \frac12.$$
The loading (here $P(i) = i/21$) is a red herring — one fair die makes the sum's parity uniform. This is the additive version of "one uniform randomizer wipes out the other's bias."

problems.title: One Specific Sequence
problems.question: 5 fair coins are flipped. Given that exactly 3 heads were obtained, find the probability that the sequence was exactly $THHTH$.
problems.hint: Given the number of heads, every arrangement of them is equally likely.
problems.solution:
Conditioned on exactly $3$ heads in $5$ flips, all $\binom{5}{3} = 10$ head-position patterns are equally likely (each unconditional sequence has the same probability $2^{-5}$). $THHTH$ is one of them:
$$P = \frac{1}{10}.$$

problems.title: Independent Family Events
problems.question: A family has $n \ge 2$ children, each independently a boy or girl with probability $1/2$. Let $X$ be the event that the family has at least one child of each gender, and $Y$ the event that there is at most one girl. Find the unique $n$ for which $X$ and $Y$ are independent.
problems.hint: Write $P(X)$, $P(Y)$, and $P(X \cap Y)$ as functions of $n$ and solve $P(X\cap Y) = P(X)P(Y)$.
problems.solution:
With $2^n$ equally likely gender sequences:
$$P(X) = 1 - \frac{2}{2^n}, \qquad P(Y) = \frac{n+1}{2^n}, \qquad P(X \cap Y) = P(\text{exactly one girl}) = \frac{n}{2^n}.$$
Independence requires $\frac{n}{2^n} = \left(1 - 2^{1-n}\right)\frac{n+1}{2^n}$, i.e. $n = (n+1)(1 - 2^{1-n})$.

Testing: $n = 3$ gives $4 \cdot \tfrac34 = 3$ ✓ (and the two sides cross only once, so it's unique).
$$n = 3.$$
This is the famous "boy–girl independence" curiosity: it works only for families of three.

problems.title: The Witness Problem
problems.question: Someone leaked IP from a quant firm! A witness claims the leaker was a trader. Witnesses are correct $2/3$ of the time, and $2/3$ of the firm's employees are traders (the rest are researchers). Given the witness's statement, what is the probability the leaker was a trader?
problems.hint: Two ways the witness says "trader": right about a trader, or wrong about a researcher.
problems.solution:
Treat the base rate as the prior that the leaker is a trader. The witness says "trader" either correctly (leaker is a trader, witness right) or incorrectly (leaker is a researcher, witness wrong):
$$P(\text{trader} \mid \text{says trader}) = \frac{\frac23\cdot\frac23}{\frac23\cdot\frac23 + \frac13\cdot\frac13} = \frac{4/9}{5/9} = \frac45.$$
The witness's testimony upgrades the base rate from $2/3$ to $4/5$ — this is the taxicab problem with friendlier numbers.

problems.title: Trapped Behind Five Doors
problems.question: Jeff is trapped in a room with 5 indistinguishable doors. Two lead to freedom via paths of 2 and 5 miles. Three lead back to the room via paths of 1, 3, and 4 miles, after which the doors are scrambled. What is the expected number of miles Jeff travels before reaching freedom?
problems.hint: Let $E$ be the answer and condition on the first door — returning doors put Jeff back in the same state.
problems.solution:
By first-step analysis, with each door equally likely:
$$E = \frac15(2) + \frac15(5) + \frac15(1 + E) + \frac15(3 + E) + \frac15(4 + E)$$
$$E = \frac{15}{5} + \frac35 E \;\Rightarrow\; \frac25 E = 3 \;\Rightarrow\; E = 7.5 \text{ miles}.$$
Quick sanity view: each "attempt" walks an average of $3$ miles and succeeds with probability $\tfrac25$, so $E = 3 \cdot \tfrac52 = 7.5$.

problems.title: Divisors of a Googol-ish Number
problems.question: A divisor is selected uniformly at random from the positive divisors of $10^{99}$. What is the probability that it is divisible by $10^{80}$?
problems.hint: Every divisor is $2^a 5^b$ with $0 \le a, b \le 99$. Which $(a,b)$ work?
problems.solution:
Divisors of $10^{99} = 2^{99}5^{99}$ are $2^a 5^b$ with $0 \le a, b \le 99$ — a $100 \times 100$ grid. Divisibility by $10^{80}$ needs $a \ge 80$ and $b \ge 80$: a $20 \times 20$ corner:
$$P = \frac{20^2}{100^2} = \frac{1}{25} = 0.04.$$

problems.title: Poisson Coin, No Heads
problems.question: Let $N \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(2)$. A fair coin is flipped $N$ times. Find the probability that no heads are obtained. (If $N = 0$, that counts as no heads.)
problems.hint: Condition on $N$ and recognize the sum as a Poisson generating function — or recall Poisson thinning.
problems.solution:
Conditioning on $N$:
$$P = \sum_{n\ge0} e^{-2}\frac{2^n}{n!}\left(\frac12\right)^n = e^{-2}\sum_{n\ge0}\frac{1^n}{n!} = e^{-2}\,e^{1} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679.$$
Slicker: by Poisson thinning, the number of heads is itself $\mathrm{Poisson}(2 \cdot \tfrac12) = \mathrm{Poisson}(1)$, so $P(\text{0 heads}) = e^{-1}$ immediately.

problems.title: The Hundredth Coin
problems.question: A pile has 100 coins: 99 fair and 1 double-headed. You pick a coin at random and flip it 10 times, observing 10 heads. What is the probability you picked the double-headed coin?
problems.hint: Odds form: prior $1{:}99$, and each head doubles the odds.
problems.solution:
Prior odds $1 : 99$; ten heads multiply the odds by $2^{10} = 1024$:
$$\text{posterior odds} = 1024 : 99 \;\Rightarrow\; P = \frac{1024}{1024 + 99} = \frac{1024}{1123} \approx 0.912.$$
Compare with the 19-fair-coins version: same machinery, and note how 10 heads still leaves a $\sim 9\%$ chance of a fair coin because the prior was so lopsided.

problems.title: The Added Baby
problems.question: A room has 5 babies: 2 boys and 3 girls. A baby of unknown sex (boy or girl with equal probability) is added. One of the 6 babies is then selected at random, and it is a boy. What is the probability that the added baby is a boy?
problems.hint: The evidence "a random pick was a boy" is more likely in the world where the added baby is a boy — quantify with Bayes.
problems.solution:
If the added baby is a boy, the room is $3$ boys / $6$ babies; if a girl, $2/6$. By Bayes:
$$P(\text{added boy} \mid \text{picked boy}) = \frac{\frac12\cdot\frac36}{\frac12\cdot\frac36 + \frac12\cdot\frac26} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6.$$
Seeing a boy tips the $50{-}50$ prior to $60{-}40$ — a clean example of evidence that feels "indirect" but still updates the prior.
